title: What is the difference between a List, a Set, and a Map in the Collections Framework?

answers.junior: List, Set, and Map are the three primary interfaces in the Java Collections Framework, each designed for distinct use cases. A List is an ordered, indexed collection that allows duplicate elements; implementations like ArrayList and LinkedList maintain insertion order and support index-based access via get(int index).
answers.middle: List, Set, and Map are the three primary interfaces in the Java Collections Framework, each designed for distinct use cases. A List is an ordered, indexed collection that allows duplicate elements; implementations like ArrayList and LinkedList maintain insertion order and support index-based access via get(int index). A Set is an unordered collection that enforces uniqueness — no duplicate elements are permitted — with HashSet providing O(1) average-time operations, LinkedHashSet maintaining insertion order, and TreeSet maintaining sorted order with O(log n) operations. A Map is not a true Collection (it doesn't extend the Collection interface) but is part of the framework; it stores key-value pairs where keys must be unique, and implementations include HashMap (O(1) average access), LinkedHashMap (insertion-ordered), and TreeMap (sorted keys). The choice between them depends on your access pattern: use List when order and index-based access matter (e.g., a playlist), use Set when you need fast uniqueness checks (e.g., a set of visited URLs), and use Map when you need key-based lookup (e.g., a cache or registry).
answers.senior: List, Set, and Map are the three primary interfaces in the Java Collections Framework, each designed for distinct use cases. A List is an ordered, indexed collection that allows duplicate elements; implementations like ArrayList and LinkedList maintain insertion order and support index-based access via get(int index). A Set is an unordered collection that enforces uniqueness — no duplicate elements are permitted — with HashSet providing O(1) average-time operations, LinkedHashSet maintaining insertion order, and TreeSet maintaining sorted order with O(log n) operations. A Map is not a true Collection (it doesn't extend the Collection interface) but is part of the framework; it stores key-value pairs where keys must be unique, and implementations include HashMap (O(1) average access), LinkedHashMap (insertion-ordered), and TreeMap (sorted keys). The choice between them depends on your access pattern: use List when order and index-based access matter (e.g., a playlist), use Set when you need fast uniqueness checks (e.g., a set of visited URLs), and use Map when you need key-based lookup (e.g., a cache or registry). Performance-wise, contains() on an ArrayList is O(n) while on a HashSet it is O(1) — a critical distinction in hot code paths. Since Java 9, factory methods like List.of(), Set.of(), and Map.of() create immutable instances, which is preferred for read-only data to improve safety and performance. All three have thread-unsafe default implementations; use Collections.synchronizedList/Set/Map wrappers or concurrent alternatives like CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap for multi-threaded environments. A subtle point: Map.Entry allows you to iterate over key-value pairs efficiently using entrySet(), which is always preferred over iterating keySet() and calling get() separately to avoid double lookups.
answers.tricky: List, Set, and Map are the three primary interfaces in the Java Collections Framework, each designed for distinct use cases. Performance-wise, contains() on an ArrayList is O(n) while on a HashSet it is O(1) — a critical distinction in hot code paths. Since Java 9, factory methods like List.of(), Set.of(), and Map.of() create immutable instances, which is preferred for read-only data to improve safety and performance. A subtle point: Map.Entry allows you to iterate over key-value pairs efficiently using entrySet(), which is always preferred over iterating keySet() and calling get() separately to avoid double lookups.
